An AI-Powered Tech Intelligence Platform for Microsoft

IE × Microsoft AI/ML Engineering Capstone — 2026

1\. Introduction

Appendix A: Project Timeline and Team Responsibilities

Appendix B: Risk Management

**Abstract**

Microsoft employees across engineering, product, legal, and leadership functions currently track a fast-moving technology landscape by manually scanning dozens of blogs, RSS feeds, and internal Slack links — an unstructured, duplicated effort repeated independently by every team. MAI News is an AI-powered tech intelligence platform that automates this process: it ingests articles from a curated set of RSS sources on a fixed schedule, removes duplicates, ranks the results against each user's stated interests, and delivers a source-grounded summary through two channels — a scheduled email digest and a conversational chatbot that answers questions using only retrieved, cited articles. The system runs on a single PostgreSQL database extended with the pgvector extension, avoiding the operational overhead of a separate vector store, and uses a pluggable large language model (LLM) client that can switch between OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google Gemini through a single configuration value. Over a ten-week MVP sprint, the team built and deployed the full pipeline to Azure, verified it end-to-end against live data (a production ingestion run indexing unique articles, and an authenticated chat session returning a correctly cited answer), and instrumented per-request token cost tracking to support the project's compliance and budget-awareness requirements. This report documents the system's design, the reasoning behind its key architectural decisions, what was verified working, what remains incomplete, and what a production deployment inside Microsoft would require next.

**1\. Introduction**

**1.1 Problem and Motivation**

Keeping up with developments in artificial intelligence, cloud infrastructure, competitor strategy, and technology regulation is a recurring, unstructured task inside Microsoft. Engineering teams, product managers, legal reviewers, and leadership all need a current picture of what changed this week in their area, but today each function re-derives that picture independently — reading the same blogs, the same press releases, and the same regulatory announcements, without a shared source of truth or a record of where a given claim came from. This duplication adds up across the organization, and without source attribution, decisions end up resting on secondhand summaries rather than the original reporting.

MAI News addresses this by building a shared intelligence layer: one pipeline ingests, filters, ranks, and summarizes relevant news once, and distributes the result to every subscribed user personalized to their stated role, region, and topic interests — with every claim traceable back to a real, cited article.

**1.2 Project Objectives**

The project was scoped as a ten-week MVP sprint with one goal: demonstrate the full pipeline end to end, from raw RSS feed to a personalized, cited output a user actually receives. Concretely, this meant:

| Objective | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Registration and personalization | a user can authenticate and declare a role, a region, and a set of topic interests. Region and topic preferences drive ranking and retrieval directly; role is currently surfaced as context inside the generation prompt rather than as a scored ranking input, a distinction discussed in Section 3.3 and revisited as a follow-up in Section 9\. |
| Automated, grounded digests | an LLM-written newsletter in which every claim is traceable to a real, cited article, delivered by email on a schedule the user controls. |
| Conversational exploration | a retrieval-augmented chatbot that answers questions about recent technology news using only the articles it retrieves, with citations. |
| Cost and compliance awareness | per-user token cost had to be measured, not assumed, and data provenance had to be documented from ingestion through display. |

**1.3 Relevance to Microsoft**

The direct value proposition is reduced duplicated effort: a shared, role- and region-aware intelligence layer means each function reads a synthesis built once, with attribution, rather than independently re-deriving the same picture. The token-cost tracking built into the architecture answers the question any internal-tooling sponsor asks first — what does this cost to run per user — with a measured figure, given in Section 6\. If extended past MVP scope, the same pipeline could plausibly serve as a shared internal tool across multiple organizational functions, provided the compliance gaps identified in Section 7 are closed first.

**2\. Data Acquisition and Source Management**

**2.1 Source Registry**

The system draws from a curated registry of RSS feeds rather than scraping arbitrary websites, keeping acquisition predictable, compliant, and easy to extend. The registry is a single JSON file, sources.json, kept at the repository root and read directly by both the ingestion pipeline and the backend's personalization layer, so a source's metadata is defined in exactly one place.

Each entry records a stable identifier, the RSS feed URL, a source type (primary, secondary, or aggregator, used as a ranking signal), a scrape tier controlling how often it is polled, a content-quality flag, and default tags across four dimensions — topic, business, regulation/policy, and region — that seed the personalization matching described in Section 3.3.

The registry currently holds more than 40 RSS sources. Paywalled sources are excluded entirely, since paywalled excerpts create both a legal exposure and a content-quality problem not worth the added volume at MVP scale. Source coverage is currently weighted toward Europe and major global English-language outlets — a direct consequence of sprint scope, with two people on data engineering and ten weeks total prioritizing a smaller, higher-quality set of sources over even coverage across regions. The registry's region taxonomy already models all seven Microsoft geographic regions, so extending coverage is a matter of adding and vetting sources, not a schema change, but it remains a real gap outside Europe and major English-language markets.

**2.2 Ingestion Pipeline**

Article ingestion follows a fixed sequence: fetch every enabled source's RSS feed, remove duplicates, screen the results with an LLM-based curation step, generate embeddings, and write the surviving articles to the database. This pipeline is implemented inside the backend service and is triggered by a scheduled GitHub Actions workflow that calls an internal, secret-protected endpoint every six hours, rather than running as part of a normal user request — so a slow or failing ingestion run never affects API availability for end users.

An earlier prototype explored a separate, standalone ingestion module, built independently against the same source registry. That module remains in the repository — some of its source-identifier conventions are still referenced by the backend for backward compatibility — but it is not part of the scheduled path that runs in production; the backend's own ingestion pipeline is what actually runs.

Within that pipeline, each enabled source is polled concurrently according to its scrape tier. A per-source watermark — the timestamp of the most recently seen article — is persisted in Postgres after every successful run, so a re-run never reprocesses articles it has already seen. After fetching, articles are deduplicated by URL and then screened by an LLM acting as an editor: given a batch of candidate headlines, it scores each for newsworthiness and keeps only the highest-scoring subset, filtering out low-value content such as press-release reposts and removing redundancy when multiple feeds cover the same story. If this step fails, the pipeline falls back to keeping every deduplicated article rather than losing the run.

Surviving articles are embedded and written to Postgres. The same scheduled run also enforces the project's one-year retention policy: any article older than 365 days is automatically deleted on every ingestion cycle.

**2.3 Compliance in Acquisition**

Because the platform redistributes summaries of third-party journalism, the data model is designed to support article provenance through source URL, ingestion timestamp, original language, RSS-feed metadata, and content-hash fields. In the MVP, the scheduled backend ingestion path reliably stores the article URL, source, ingestion timestamp, and detected language giving a full audit trail for anything the system has ever surfaced. One gap remains: the data model includes a field for each source's licensing terms, but the source registry itself does not yet carry that information for any entry, so the field exists in schema but is not populated. This is addressed further in Section 7\.

**3\. System Architecture and Methodology**

**3.1 Architecture Overview**

The system is organized into four stages.

| Stage | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Ingestion | polls RSS sources on a schedule, curates, deduplicates, embeds, and persists watermark state. |
| Storage | is the canonical store for articles, users, preferences, digests, and embeddings, implemented as PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server. |
| Processing | tags, embeds, ranks, and summarizes articles per user profile using a pluggable LLM client, a self-hosted embedding model, and an in-house ranking formula. |
| Serving | exposes digests, chat, and preferences over a REST API, implemented as a FastAPI backend on Azure Container Apps serving a Next.js frontend hosted as a static export on Azure Storage. |

**3.2 Data Layer**

Production storage is PostgreSQL 16 with the pgvector extension, hosted on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server in the francecentral region. This consolidates what was originally a two-system design: an earlier version used ChromaDB as a separate vector store alongside a relational database for users and preferences. Combining both into one Postgres instance means ranking queries that mix semantic similarity with relational filters — region, topic overlap, recency — run as a single query rather than requiring the application to merge results from two separate systems. A configuration entry and package dependency from the earlier ChromaDB setup remain in the repository but are no longer used by anything that runs in production.

Before any application code was built against pgvector, its availability was confirmed with a live connection test covering the extension, the schema, embedding dimensions, and a full insert, read, and delete cycle.

**3.3 AI/ML Components**

| Component | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Tagging | Articles are tagged across four dimensions:  topic, business, regulation/policy, and region. They are  inherited at curated source-level default tags. |
| Embeddings | The default embedding model is all-MiniLM-L6-v2, a small, self-hosted model that runs with no external API call and no per-embedding cost. A Gemini embedding alternative is also available behind a configuration switch, though switching requires re-embedding the entire existing article corpus, since the two models produce vectors of different sizes. |
| Ranking | Each article is scored against a user's profile using a weighted formula combining five signals: semantic similarity to the user's interests, tag overlap across the four taxonomy dimensions, whether a tracked company is mentioned, recency, and source quality. These weights can be overridden per user. An external reranking service was evaluated but not adopted, since the in-house formula met MVP needs without adding another dependency. |
| Generation | The newsletter and chatbot are both written by a pluggable LLM client that can run on OpenAI, Anthropic Claude, or Google Gemini through a single configuration setting, with OpenAI as the default. Prompt caching and per-turn token cost tracking were both built in to control spend, given the project's zero-budget constraint. |

User role is not one of these signals. Region and topic preferences reach ranking directly through tag overlap; role instead appears only as context inside the generation prompt, since the team had not yet agreed on a stable role taxonomy to score against when this was built. 

**3.4 Backend and Frontend**

The backend is a FastAPI REST API exposing registration, preferences, digest history, chat, and admin-only internal endpoints, deployed as a Docker container on Azure Container Apps. Authentication runs through Microsoft Entra ID OAuth in multi-tenant mode, alongside a passwordless email magic-link flow as a fallback. A Google OAuth integration exists in the codebase but is not currently active.

The frontend is a Next.js and Tailwind application built as a static export, hosted on an Azure Storage static website and calling the live API directly.

Digest emails are sent through Azure Communication Services. Delivery is per-user and timezone-aware — each user's preferences record a delivery day, hour, and timezone, and the digest worker evaluates eligibility against each user's own local time rather than a single fixed send time.

The backend is containerized via Docker and deployed to Azure Container Apps; a Render.com configuration also exists in the repository as an alternative deployment target.

![][image3]

**4\. Key Design Decisions**

**4.1 Unified Data Store: PostgreSQL \+ pgvector**

The original design used ChromaDB as a dedicated vector store alongside a separate relational database. The team consolidated onto PostgreSQL with pgvector instead, because at this project's scale, operating one database was simpler than keeping two systems schema-consistent, and it let ranking queries that combine similarity search with relational filters run as a single query. The trade-off is that a dedicated vector database would offer faster retrieval at a much larger scale than this project reaches.

**4.2 Pluggable Multi-Provider LLM Client**

Rather than committing to a single LLM vendor, the team built one client interface with three interchangeable implementations — OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google Gemini — selected through a single configuration value, with OpenAI as the default. This cost more integration work to build and maintain than a single-vendor approach, but it let the team compare cost and quality across providers during development and avoids being locked into one vendor's pricing or availability. One known defect in this design is that the component estimating dollar cost per call currently applies Anthropic's pricing regardless of which provider actually served the request; this is addressed in Sections 6 and 7\.

**4.3 Other Alternatives Considered**

A few smaller decisions followed the same pattern:

| Area | Decision |
| :---- | :---- |
| Azure Database for PostgreSQL | was chosen over Neon, Aiven, or a self-managed virtual machine for its free tier and confirmed pgvector support. |
| GitHub Actions cron | was chosen over Airflow since the project only needed two recurring jobs. |
| An external reranking API | was evaluated and passed over in favor of the in-house ranking formula. |
| Azure Container Apps | was chosen over Render.com to keep all infrastructure on one cloud provider, with the Render configuration kept as a documented fallback. |

**5\. Implementation**

This section summarizes how the concepts described in Sections 2 through 4 are actually implemented.

**5.1 Source Registry and Personalization Matching**

The backend loads the source registry once per process and matches each user against sources whose tags overlap with their declared interests across any of the four taxonomy dimensions. Primary AI/ML sources are also surfaced to any user with an AI/ML interest even without an exact tag match, since the team judged that category central enough to the product to include by default.

**5.2 Incremental, Scheduled Ingestion**

The ingestion worker restores each source's watermark, fetches all enabled sources concurrently, deduplicates, curates, embeds, stores the results, and enforces the retention policy, all within a single scheduled run triggered by the internal endpoint described in Section 2.2. If any component fails to initialize, the run reports itself as unavailable rather than failing the request that triggered it.

**5.3 LLM-Based Curation**

The curation step sends the LLM a compact list of candidate titles and sources and asks it to score each for newsworthiness, keeping only the top-scoring subset above a minimum quality threshold. If the call fails or returns an unusable response, the pipeline keeps every candidate rather than losing the run.

**5.4 Embeddings**

Embeddings are generated once, at ingestion time, using the self-hosted model described in Section 3.3. Output dimensions are checked against the configured value before being written to the database, to catch any mismatch early.

**5.5 Personalized Ranking**

The ranking formula described in Section 3.3 is applied to every candidate article before it is shown or sent to a user, with per-user weight overrides normalized so they always sum to one.

**5.6 Grounded Generation and Conversational Chat**

Both the newsletter and the chatbot retrieve relevant articles first and generate an answer only from that retrieved set, citing the source for each claim, with an explicit fallback response stating that the available retrieved articles do not support an answer when no relevant context is found.

For chat, the user's query is embedded and matched against the article corpus using vector similarity search, retrieving up to twenty candidates; if that returns nothing, the system falls back to the most recently ingested articles. Results are capped at three articles per source so the retrieved context isn't dominated by a single publisher. Conversation history is stored per session and reinjected on each new message, trimmed to the six most recent turns, so the chatbot can handle follow-up questions without the user repeating context — though this means prompt length, and cost, grows with conversation length, quantified in Section 6\.

This flow was verified against the live deployment: an authenticated chat session returned a fully streamed, correctly cited answer grounded in real articles, and correctly declined to answer from general knowledge when a follow-up question had no matching retrieved context.

**5.7 API Surface and Authentication**

The backend exposes authentication, preferences, article browsing, streaming chat, digest history, saved articles, folder organization, and admin-only internal endpoints, with separate liveness and readiness health checks used by Azure Container Apps. Token usage is recorded per chat turn in the database; the dollar-cost estimate computed from it is accurate only when Claude is the active provider, since it currently uses Anthropic's pricing regardless of which provider served the call. Section 6 quantifies the effect of this.

**6\. Evaluation and Results**

The pipeline was evaluated against live, production data rather than only local test fixtures.

Ingestion. A production run indexed 409 unique articles in a single pass, confirming the full ingest sequence runs correctly under the actual production schedule and infrastructure.

Retrieval and generation. An authenticated chat session, run through the same login flow a real user would use, returned a grounded, correctly cited answer citing twenty real articles, recording 3,635 prompt tokens and 486 completion tokens for that turn. A separate, narrower follow-up question with no matching retrieved context produced the intended fallback behavior — an explicit statement that the available articles didn't cover the question — rather than an unlabeled answer from general knowledge.

Cost. The system's configured default model is not GPT-4o; it is a smaller model priced at $0.20 per million input tokens and $1.25 per million output tokens. At that rate, the measured turn above costs approximately $0.0013. Using an illustrative estimate of one digest plus three chats per day, roughly 120 calls per month, this projects to approximately $0.16 per active user per month.

The application's own displayed cost estimate would report a different number for the same turn: because it applies Anthropic's rate regardless of active provider, it computes approximately $0.018 — about fourteen times the real cost under the system's actual configuration. Fixing this means reading the rate for whichever provider served the call, since the correct per-provider rates already exist in the code; it is the most immediately useful fix identified during evaluation.

GPT-4o remains available as a higher-quality, higher-cost option through the same configuration switch. Because conversation memory reinjects prior turns into every call, the measured $0.0013 figure reflects a single early-session turn rather than a steady-state cost across a longer conversation; no mechanism yet caps this growth.

Embeddings and ranking are effectively free at any volume, since both run without an external API call.

| Metric | Value |
| :---- | :---- |
| Articles indexed (production run) | 409 |
| Prompt tokens (sample chat turn) | 3,635 |
| Completion tokens (sample chat turn) | 486 |
| Measured cost per turn (actual) | $0.0013 |
| Displayed cost per turn (buggy estimate) | $0.018 |
| Projected cost per active user / month | $0.16 |

**7\. Limitations and Known Gaps**

Several aspects of the system are incomplete or contain known defects. They are listed together here rather than scattered through the report.

| Issue | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Cost-estimation bug | The application's displayed per-call cost hardcodes Anthropic's pricing regardless of the active provider, overstating real cost by roughly fourteen times under the current configuration. The fix is small: read the correct rate for whichever provider served the call, using pricing constants that already exist in the code. |
| Source-level licensing metadata | Each source record has a field for licensing terms, but the source registry carries no such data for any entry, so the field is unpopulated. This is needed before the platform can give a defensible answer about redistribution rights. |
| Account deletion | The account-deletion endpoint soft-deletes a user's account immediately. A scheduled job to hard-delete the row after 24 hours is documented but does not exist in the codebase, so the soft-deleted state is currently permanent. |
| No request rate limiting | Chat and internal trigger endpoints have no per-user or per-IP request limit, which is a real exposure to both abuse and unbounded LLM cost, particularly given that conversation memory makes long sessions more expensive over time. |
| Automated deployment | Automated CI/CD deployment is implemented but has never run successfully, since it requires a tenant-level permission no team member holds. The team's working alternative — a manual deploy using an individual account's existing permissions — has been used successfully multiple times. |

None of these blocked the MVP's core demonstrated capability, but each should be weighed against what works when evaluating production readiness.

**8\. Conclusion**

**8.1 Summary of Technical Achievements**

By the end of the sprint, the pipeline runs end to end against real, live data: ingestion pulls live articles on schedule, deduplication and curation reduce them to a higher-quality set, embedding and storage populate a single Postgres and pgvector database, ranking produces a personalized order per user, and generation produces a cited newsletter and chatbot response grounded in that set. Section 6 confirmed this directly: a production run indexed 409 articles, and a real chat session returned a grounded, correctly cited answer with a fully measured cost.

Consolidating the data layer onto Postgres with pgvector simplified the system in practice, letting ranking queries run as a single query and removing the risk of two schemas drifting apart. The pluggable LLM client also proved its value during evaluation: it let the team discover that the system's real per-user cost is roughly ten times lower than what its own cost display currently reports.

Section 7 named five specific, unresolved items — the cost-display bug, unpopulated source licensing, the missing account hard-deletion job, the absence of rate limiting, and the blocked automated-deployment path. None of these block the MVP's demonstrated capability, but each should be weighed when assessing how close the system is to production-ready.

**8.2 Business Implications**

For Microsoft, the core value proposition is reduced duplicated effort: a shared, attributed intelligence layer instead of each team re-deriving the same picture independently. Section 6 gives that a concrete number — approximately $0.16 per active user per month, dominated by LLM usage and bounded further by free embeddings and a zero-cost ranking formula. This is small enough that operating cost is not the limiting factor for a pilot; the governance gaps in Section 7, particularly source licensing and account deletion, are.

The pluggable provider architecture is a genuine asset past MVP: since OpenAI is already the default model family, migrating to Microsoft's own Azure OpenAI Service would be a configuration change rather than a rewrite. That same flexibility doesn't extend to the embedding model, which would require re-embedding the existing article corpus if switched.

**9\. Future Work**

Beyond the specific fixes named in Section 7, several infrastructure improvements would be necessary before a production deployment inside Microsoft. Several of the deployment and authentication constraints encountered during this sprint are specific to the academic context — the use of IE University student Microsoft accounts restricted access to certain Azure subscription-level permissions. In a real production deployment, the platform would run under the organization's own Azure tenant and credentials, with full access to managed identities, CI/CD integrations, and enterprise-grade configuration from the outset.

| Area | Improvement Needed |
| :---- | :---- |
| Eliminating cold starts | The Container App currently scales to zero when idle, incurring a ten-to-twenty-second startup delay on the first request after a period of inactivity. Keeping one instance always warm would remove this delay, at the cost of continuous compute billing during idle periods. |
| Email delivery reliability | Azure Communication Services, used for both magic-link authentication and newsletter delivery, encountered repeated transient failures during development. A production deployment would benefit from a queue-based retry mechanism rather than direct, unretried calls to the email service. |
| Observability and alerting | No automated alerting currently exists; failed digest runs, email delivery failures, and unusual LLM latency are discovered manually. Integrating a monitoring and alerting service would close this gap. |
| Database scaling | The current PostgreSQL instance runs on a small burstable tier. As the article corpus and query volume grow, pgvector similarity search under real concurrent load would likely require a larger instance tier or a connection pooler. |
| Network isolation | The backend API is currently publicly reachable with no network boundary between it and the database. A production deployment should place both inside a private network, reducing the externally-facing attack surface to the API layer alone. |
| CDN and custom domain | The static frontend is served directly from storage with no content delivery network in front of it. Adding one would provide edge caching, enforced HTTPS on a custom domain, and baseline protection against traffic spikes. |
| Secrets rotation | Application secrets are centralized in a secrets manager, but no rotation policy is currently configured for any of them. A production deployment should attach rotation policies to all secrets, particularly the database connection string and LLM API keys. |
| Multi-region resilience | All infrastructure currently runs in a single Azure region with no failover path. A production deployment would need, at minimum, geo-redundant database backups and a documented recovery time objective. |
| Cost governance | No automated spending caps or budget alerts currently exist. Configuring budget alerts with notification thresholds is a low-effort addition against runaway spend from a compromised key or a misbehaving job. |

Application-level follow-ups, carried forward from Section 7: correcting the cost-estimation logic to use per-provider pricing; populating source-level licensing metadata; implementing the documented account hard-deletion job; and adding per-user or per-IP rate limiting to the chat and internal trigger endpoints.

**Appendix A: Project Timeline and Team Responsibilities**

The sprint was planned in five two-week phases, each building a working, testable slice of the pipeline before the next stage began:

| Phase | Focus |
| :---- | :---- |
| 1 | Discovery and architecture |
| 2 | Ingestion and storage |
| 3 | Processing |
| 4 | Serving |
| 5 | Hardening and reporting, ending with end-to-end verification against the live deployed system and this report. |

Work was divided by functional area so each person owned a vertical slice end to end:

| Person | Responsibilities |
| :---- | :---- |
| Ines Villarino de Funes | Led source registry design and data compliance/retention policy. |
| Kimberly Bertoli | Led the ingestion pipeline and served as repository owner. |
| Joaquin Fernandez Sande | Led LLM integration, grounded generation, and cost tracking. |
| Aspen Nicolas | Led the backend API, authentication, and deployment. |
| Irene Gracia | Led the data layer and ranking implementation. |
| Philine de Graaf | Led the frontend build and deployment. |
| Valeria Gaona | Led the chatbot interface and personalization forms. |

**Appendix B: Risk Management**

Risk was tracked informally through the sprint.

| Risk | Mitigation |
| :---- | :---- |
| Vendor lock-in and pricing risk | were mitigated up front by building the pluggable LLM client before committing to a single vendor. |
| A single-writer data race between concurrent ingestion and read access | was avoided by a strict rule that only the ingestion pipeline writes to article-related tables. |
| An institutional permissions blocker | prevented automated CI/CD deployment; the team's mitigation was a manual deploy workflow using an individually held Contributor role, used successfully multiple times. |
| The cost-estimation bug | once discovered, was addressed directly: the team computed the Section 6 cost figures independently from raw recorded token counts and flagged the bug itself as a scoped fix. |
| Scope creep on personalization | was addressed by descoping full role-weighted ranking mid-sprint once it became clear the role taxonomy itself was not yet agreed on, keeping role as prompt context instead and moving the fuller version to Section 9 as a follow-up. |
